title: Psalms 42
subtitle: As the Deer Pants for Streams of Water

heb.paragraph: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God. My soul thirsts (ṣāməʾâ) for God, for the living God. When can I go and meet with God?" (vv.1–2). Book II opens (Psalms 42–72, the "Elohistic Psalter" where God is called Elohim rather than YHWH). A Son of Korah psalm. The psalmist is exiled from the temple, thirsting for God’s presence. "My tears have been my food day and night, while people say to me all day long, ‘Where is your God?’" (v.3). The refrain: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5).

notes.note: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God." The most famous thirst-image in Scripture. Physical dehydration as metaphor for spiritual longing.
"Where is your God?" The taunt of the enemy. The absent God is the psalmist’s deepest wound.

"Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him, my Saviour and my God." The refrain (repeated in 42:11 and 43:5): self-counsel. The psalmist preaches to his own soul.

heb.paragraph: "Deep calls to deep (təhōm) in the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have swept over me" (v.7). The image of being overwhelmed: wave upon wave. The deeps of suffering call to each other across the psalmist’s soul. Yet: "By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me" (v.8). Day-love and night-song — even in the flood, God’s love and music persist.

notes.note: "Deep calls to deep." The waves are God’s — "YOUR waves and breakers." Even the overwhelming flood belongs to God. The suffering is not random; it is under divine jurisdiction.
"By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me." The most remarkable claim: in the deepest water, God sends both love and music. Day and night, the provisions arrive.
"Why, my soul, are you downcast?" The refrain returns. The psalmist has not been rescued; he is still in exile. But the self-counsel continues: hope in God.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI say to God my Rock, “Why have you forgotten me? Why must I go about mourning, oppressed by the enemy?”ESVI say to God, my rock: \u201cWhy have you forgotten me? Why do I go mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?\u201dNIVThese things I remember as I pour out my soul: how I used to go to the house of God under the protection of the Mighty One with shouts of joy and praise among the festive throng.ESVThese things I remember, as I pour out my soul: how I would go with the throng and lead them in procession to the house of God with glad shouts and songs of praise, a multitude keeping festival.

rec.text: ""As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God." The most famous thirst-image in Scripture. Physical dehydration as metaphor for spiritual longing."

rows.label: Deep Calls to Deep — 42:6–11
rows.text: Waves and breakers. Day-love, night-song. The refrain: "Why are you downcast, my soul?"

hebtext.note: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God. My soul thirsts (ṣāməʾâ) for God, for the living God. When can I go and meet with God?" (vv.1–2). Book II opens (Psalms 42–72, the "Elohistic Psalter" where God is called Elohim rather than YHWH). A Son of Korah psalm. The psalmist is exiled from the temple, thirsting for God’s presence. "My tears have been my food day and night, while people say to me all day long, ‘Where is your God?’" (v.3). The refrain: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5).

"Deep calls to deep (təhōm) in the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have swept over me" (v.7). The image of being overwhelmed: wave upon wave. The deeps of suffering call to each other across the psalmist’s soul. Yet: "By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me" (v.8). themes.note: Faith peaks: self-counsel in depression — "put your hope in God." Mercy: day-love and night-song in the flood. Worship: Book II opens with longing for God’s presence.
